summary: Riccomini analyzes Confluent's WarpStream acquisition through the lens of a broader industry shift toward flexible deployment options. He argues that modern infrastructure must support multiple deployment models—embedded, single-node, clustered, SaaS, BYOC, and self-hosted—rather than forcing customers into a single approach. Using ClickHouse as the exemplar, he shows how one system can scale from an in-process library to distributed clusters while supporting all deployment modes. The post identifies technical enablers like zero-disk architecture, Rust/C++ adoption, composable data systems, and WebAssembly that make this flexibility achievable. His conclusion is stark: infrastructure vendors without flexible deployment options will fail.
key_points: Confluent's WarpStream acquisition makes strategic sense because Kora (their SaaS engine) cannot easily support BYOC deployment, while WarpStream can
The ideal approach is building a single system that accommodates flexible deployment rather than maintaining multiple codebases like Confluent now must
ClickHouse exemplifies the future: it runs embedded, single-node, clustered, self-hosted, BYOC, and as SaaS—all from one codebase
The flexible deployment trend is spreading across the industry: pglite for PostgreSQL, Neon's object storage tier, MotherDuck scaling DuckDB to the cloud
Technical advances enabling this shift include zero-disk architecture, Rust/C++ adoption, composable data systems, and WebAssembly
money_quotes: Acquisition motivation and communication are rarely the same.
While there's no one-size-fits-all deployment offering, I suspect we can still build a single system that can accommodate flexible deployment.
I really didn't understand why everyone was so excited about ClickHouse until I dug into it. Then it clicked: you can run ClickHouse as a single node.
In hindsight, it's a comically obvious gap in the realtime online analytical processing (OLAP) space.
It scales from a single in-process library all the way to a distributed cluster. This is the future.

Infrastructure vendors need to ask themselves: does our system deploy self-managed, BYOC, and as a SaaS cloud? Can we support in-process embedded, single-node, and clustered execution? If not, you're cooked.
key_insight: Modern infrastructure must be built from the ground up to support the full spectrum of deployment options—from embedded libraries to distributed clusters to multi-tenant SaaS—or risk obsolescence.
